chapters.title: How the race of Jurek's life started
chapters.text:
Scott Jurek began life as a shy kid with a rare case of high blood pressure. He grew into a thin teenager who was teased and bullied for his small build. Several classmates outpaced him in body and brain. He was the sort of kid you might pass in the school hallway every day and never notice. That's how unremarkable he was. So, to carve out a corner for himself, Jurek set out on a journey of self-discovery.

The arc of Jurek's life follows a familiar pattern that many people share. It shows how many of us have to face storms, roadblocks, and challenges on the path to what we want. Not everyone has run a cross-country race or braved the heat of Death Valley, but a lot of us can still relate, learn, and feel encouraged by Jurek's life.

The longer and farther I ran, the more I realized that what I was often chasing was a state of mind, Scott Jurek has said.

There were stretches when the dust of the canyon was Jurek's closest companion, and that is why his story is worth reading. When you are going through life's emotional and physical lows, thinking about the lessons from Jurek's hard times can help you find your way forward.

Each of us has the strength to chase the uncertain.

This piece is not a collection of made-up parables, or stories to pump up your inspiration. It is also not a sermon to build your special faith for big achievements. Instead, it is a guide that points out the inevitability of life's hurdles and gives directions on beating them within yourself before stepping into the wider field of life. Are you a hairdresser, programmer, entrepreneur, or athlete? No matter your age, race, status, or background, you can prepare yourself to reach some of the hardest goals in the world. Read this summary to the end and watch as you transform from the inside out.

chapters.title: Youth does not always wear laurels
chapters.text:
When Jurek was in his late teens, his mom was hit with a bout of multiple sclerosis. The list of household chores that Jurek and his siblings had to do got longer. As the oldest child, he ended up shouldering a larger share of the tiring housework. With all those duties came a tight, wound-up feeling, and Jurek slowly slid into perfectionism. Then, at a routine check-up, he was diagnosed with high blood pressure at the young age of twelve.

That brush with high blood pressure opened Jurek's eyes to the power of the mind over the body, and to the way food can shape recovery. He could not name those ideas at the time. After the diagnosis, his dad decided to cut him some slack and let him have more free time, like the other kids.

A tough childhood can become a launching pad to greatness if it is guided the right way.

Then, in his senior year of high school, Jurek met a kid with odd personality traits. Dusty Olson would never have come to Jurek's attention if he had not been the best athlete of his age in Minnesota. Because of Olson's wild and carefree nature, he and Jurek were total opposites. Jurek secretly envied Olson, because he did not have the freedom and ease that Olson enjoyed. Jurek would learn a lot from his new friend as they later trained together for skiing competitions. Olson invited Jurek to his first competitive race, the 1994 50-mile Minnesota Voyageur, which Olson had won the year before.

However, Olson finished the 1994 Voyageur in third place, behind Jurek, who came second. Olson tripped midway through the race and lost ground to Jurek, who had been at his heels for most of it. With his skiing career almost ending because of college life and trouble balancing other parts of life, Jurek had a wake-up call that summer about which path to take. That path was running. The ski career had been Jurek's main focus, and running was just a way to stay in shape. In his second year of physical therapy training, Jurek would later flip the focus. He practiced skiing to stay in shape for ultramarathon races.

chapters.title: A childhood that became a runway
chapters.text:
When Jurek was in his late teens, his mom was hit with a bout of multiple sclerosis. The list of household chores that Jurek and his siblings had to do got longer. As the oldest child, he ended up shouldering a larger share of the tiring housework. With all those duties came a tight, wound-up feeling, and Jurek slowly slid into perfectionism. Then, at a routine check-up, he was diagnosed with high blood pressure at the young age of twelve.

That brush with high blood pressure opened Jurek's eyes to the power of the mind over the body, and to the way food can shape recovery. He could not name those ideas at the time. After the diagnosis, his dad decided to cut him some slack and let him have more free time, like the other kids.

A tough childhood can become a launching pad to greatness if it is guided the right way.

Then, in his senior year of high school, Jurek met a kid with odd personality traits. Dusty Olson would never have come to Jurek's attention if he had not been the best athlete of his age in Minnesota. Because of Olson's wild and carefree nature, he and Jurek were total opposites. Jurek secretly envied Olson, because he did not have the freedom and ease that Olson enjoyed. Jurek would learn a lot from his new friend as they later trained together for skiing competitions. Olson invited Jurek to his first competitive race, the 1994 50-mile Minnesota Voyageur, which Olson had won the year before.

However, Olson finished the 1994 Voyageur in third place, behind Jurek, who came second. Olson tripped midway through the race and lost ground to Jurek, who had been at his heels for most of it. With his skiing career almost ending because of college life and trouble balancing other parts of life, Jurek had a wake-up call that summer about which path to take. That path was running. The ski career had been Jurek's main focus, and running was just a way to stay in shape. In his second year of physical therapy training, Jurek would later flip the focus. He practiced skiing to stay in shape for ultramarathon races.

chapters.title: Independence, a go at the world
chapters.text:
On one of the occasions when Jurek had gone out with Olson, he came home late and clashed with his dad. Since he had a job and was already in college, he got his own place. After that, he visited home only now and then, when he was sure his dad would be at work. Then, in the spring of 1995, his mom's multiple sclerosis got so much worse that she had to move to a nursing home.

The news of his mom's move to a nursing home hit Jurek hard. From then on, he studied and ran harder. Before that, he knew his place during training runs with Olson, always trailing behind his running mentor. After the news about his mom, he threw his energy into athletics and school. Despite all the gains he had made going into his second Voyageur, he still finished the race in second place, with Olson not running this time. Then Jurek suddenly discovered the reason he could run as fast as he wanted. It was his diet. He remembered how strong he had felt and how well-trained he had been at the Team Birkie ski camp in Cable, Wisconsin, during his senior year of high school. At that camp, participants were fed a mostly plant-based diet.

After reading several books on healthy eating and meeting sick people during his St. Scholastica college internship at a hospital in Ashland, Wisconsin, Jurek leaned toward a plant-based diet. He ate more plants and less meat the following summer, and won the Voyageur race on his next try. With this new secret, Jurek launched his lifelong racing career with a steady, firm commitment to a mostly plant-based diet.

Showing up to train on a steady schedule pays off, with consistent progress fueling the motivation to keep coming back.

chapters.title: Food, relationships, and the person you become
chapters.text:
While Jurek was at St. Scholastica, he bumped into a young woman named Leah, a familiar face at a McDonald's he stopped by often. Leah stood out at a fast-food joint because she was fussy about what she ate, with choices that ran from diet Coke to pricey organic fruits.

The pair tied the knot at her parents' home on August 17, 1996, capping a friendship that felt like it had been going on forever. In truth, they simply shared common interests and felt a pull toward each other from their very first meeting.

Jurek's mom was there for the wedding. His dad had thoughtfully picked her up from the nursing home. Dusty Olson also showed up. Unlike his wild self, he managed to put on a suit and tie. Not long after Jurek and Leah got married, his parents split up. His mom had suggested the split after moving to a nursing home at forty-four, not wanting to feel like a burden.

The circle of friends you keep shapes how you see life.

Jurek's second year of physical therapy internship was in Seattle. Leah stayed behind while he lived in a hostel to save money. Unlike the meat-heavy meals common in Duluth, Minnesota, Jurek's time in Seattle pulled him closer to a plant-based diet. Still, he had the chance to grab fast food several times a week. That changed when Jurek got his first full-time job as a physical therapist and had to move with Leah to Deadwood, South Dakota. They moved in April 1998. Deadwood was an isolated town, and the fast food that had been a short walk in Duluth was now a twenty-minute drive. So Jurek went a step further and fully embraced a plant-based diet. The couple had a garden in the yard where they grew vegetables. Having learned to cook from a young age, Jurek created new recipes while playing with old vegetarian and vegan ones.

Food, diet, and the love of healthy eating brought Jurek and Leah together. It would later shape the rhythm and shape of their lives, Jurek's in particular.

chapters.title: Getting enough protein from a plant-based diet
chapters.text:
When Jurek first thought about going vegan, his biggest worry was getting enough protein. An ultrarunner needs a balanced diet and plenty of every key nutrient. Since meat is the usual protein source in most diets, Jurek decided to lean on some less common alternatives.

He often made smoothies for breakfast, mixing in nuts and a good amount of plant-based protein powder, like pea, brown rice, fermented soy, or hemp. Sometimes he ate sprouted whole-grain toast with nut butter, or sprouted-grain cereal or porridge. Jurek usually had a big raw salad for lunch, often adding protein with a soy product such as tofu, edamame, or tempeh, some leftover cooked grain or quinoa, or a generous scoop of hummus.

A travel blender is handy for plant-based meal experiments on the go.

Dinner often included simple dishes like whole grains, beans, or pasta made with whole-grain flour. Jurek would add a soy product at dinner if he had not had one at lunch. Trail mixes and Clif Bars were regular snacks, along with some soy or nut-based vegan desserts during the day. With these and similar food choices, Jurek kept up a good supply of protein while staying away from meat, enough to maintain muscle and recover after races.

In most of the places Jurek traveled, he would buy traditional whole foods from local markets or farm shops instead of leaning on refined flour and meat substitutes. He always looked for products that had been soaked, fermented, or sprouted to make sure that the hard-to-digest cellulose in plant cell walls had been broken down. Jurek's favorite soy foods are sprouted tofu, miso, and tempeh, all easier to digest than isolated soy protein. He does not eat bread made with refined flour, and instead makes a wholemeal blend for himself from sprouted grains. Even when he eats cooked whole grains or beans, Jurek always soaks them before cooking.

chapters.title: Learning the high ropes
chapters.text:
By December 1998, Jurek and his wife had moved to Duluth, where he reconnected with Dusty Olson and his old friend Hippie Dan. That fall, Olson introduced Jurek to Jess and Katie Koski, local Minnesota athletes. The whole group ended up training and skiing together. The Koskis had heard from Olson about Jurek's racing wins and adventurous diet. So they handed him Howard Lyman's book Mad Cowboy. In the book, Lyman shares his belief that industrially farmed fish, meat, and dairy poison the body, pollute the earth, and sicken the soul.

The content of what you eat matters just as much as the act of eating.

Jurek was reading books filled with expert nutritional advice, and that was another step toward an all-plant diet. His food was mostly fresh fruits and vegetables, beans, nuts, seeds, whole grains, and soy-based foods like miso, tofu, and tempeh. Still, he sometimes veered from this routine. The few times he ate animal-based food were out of need, not a lack of personal belief or discipline.

Jurek first ran a 100-mile race in 1998, the Angeles Crest 100. Olson paced him in that race, finishing second behind Ben Hian, a three-time winner. Jurek and Leah later moved to Seattle, where Mount Si became a good training spot for the Western States 100, another 100-mile race held in California. At the time, the Western States 100 was the oldest and most respected trail ultramarathon. Jurek was going for it at age twenty-five. There were no manuals or guides for long-distance running, and the Internet was just picking up. So Jurek taught himself the Bushido principle of Japanese warriors by reading. The Bushido principle reflects the spirit of ancient Japanese warriors, with an emphasis on simplicity, honor, bravery, and self-sacrifice.

As Olson and Jurek's other training partners noticed, his endurance grew during his two months of training in Seattle. He kept eating the way that worked, with the occasional slip.

chapters.title: Riding on the wings of naysayers
chapters.text:
After his first Western States 100 win, Jeff Dean, a regular at Jurek's workplace, congratulated him but hinted that the win was a fluke. In response, Jurek entered the Western States 100 again on the spot, just to prove the doubters wrong.

He started doing yoga and reading about Buddhism to bring his mind, body, and soul together. After his second Western States win, Jurek dug deeper into Bushido by reading books like The Marathon Monks of Mount Hiei by John Stevens.

You only grow as a human being when you step outside your comfort zone, as Percy Cerutty once put it.

Jurek won a fifth straight Western States 100 in 2003, setting a new record. Sadly, Tonto, his trail dog, died that year. Most ultrarunners take at least two months off after a big race. But Jurek was undaunted. Just two weeks after winning his seventh Western States 100 in 2005, he flew to Las Vegas and signed up for the 135-mile Badwater Ultramarathon.

Jurek's first time around people from the Tarahumara tribe was at the Angeles Crest in 1998. After that, he was able to spend time with these Mexican Indian tribes in their own land and even got to race some of their very best runners. This all happened in 2006 at the Copper Canyon Ultramarathon. He had first been invited by email from Micah True, also called Caballo Blanco. A nudge from Chris McDougall, who was writing Born to Run, a study of the Tarahumara, helped him give the idea serious thought.

In late 2006, Jurek agreed to pace Brian Morrison, instead of running the race himself, for what could have been Morrison's first Western States 100 win. But Jurek collapsed about three hundred yards from the finish line and was then disqualified for being helped across. He felt terrible about the decision and even worse when rumors started in the press that he had planned Morrison's disqualification.

Criticism and pain, whether helpful or not, can be turned toward something positive.

Worth noting: British researchers have found that listening to music can ease pain about as well as taking an extra-strength Tylenol pill.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Ultramarathons call for steady confidence, held in check with a healthy dose of humility. Champions rarely lose the belief that they can outrank the competition. But they also need to accept the need for total commitment to winning. By contrast, an untrained focus, a small lapse in judgment, and a low tank of mojo are likely to end in defeat or worse.

Ultrarunners often start races at sunrise and go through sunset, moonrise, and another full turn of the earth. Sometimes they stumble from exhaustion and find themselves fighting through pain. Other times they flow down rocky trails and sprint up a 3,000-foot hill after tapping into a source of strength they couldn't reach before. As a result, ultrarunners sometimes race with sore muscles, bruised knees, and scraped skin. It is a complicated but simple math. Run until you can't go on. Then keep at it and take another step. Tap into your willpower, and new sources of energy will appear.

Jurek hit an all-time low in 2008 but kept pushing forward. His relationship with his best friend, Olson, broke down. Leah filed for divorce. His mother passed away. He had a string of losses and dropouts from several races. Jurek admitted he almost quit along the way, but by reaching into his willpower, a fresh supply of energy came alive inside him.

Even with all the techniques and strategies mentioned, an athlete's diet CANNOT be brushed aside. Looking at Scott Jurek, we can see that the food he ate had a big effect on his ultramarathon performance. The lifestyle of the Tarahumara tribe also shows how important diet is in athletics.

Try this.

To help the best in your life come through, learn from Scott Jurek and follow in his footsteps.

Checking in with yourself often makes everything else fall into place.

Being new to a field is no excuse for doing less than your best. Make the effort. Practice. Try again and again.

Learn from the very best, directly or indirectly, and you may go past your mentors.

Read it till the end?
